does: This paper introduces MultiBreak, a large-scale benchmark containing 10,389 multi-turn adversarial prompts spanning 2,665 distinct harmful intents designed to evaluate safety vulnerabilities in large language models. The authors develop an active learning pipeline that iteratively fine-tunes a generator model to produce stronger jailbreak candidates, guided by uncertainty-based refinement to maximize diversity and quality. The benchmark demonstrates substantially higher attack success rates compared to existing datasets, achieving up to 54.0 percentage points improvement over the second-best dataset on DeepSeek-R1-7B and 34.6 points on GPT-4.1-mini, while revealing that diverse attack categories expose previously undetected safety vulnerabilities.
connects: This work extends prior research on LLM safety evaluation and adversarial attack generation. It builds on single-turn jailbreak benchmarks and earlier multi-turn work by recognizing that conversational interactions provide more natural pathways to bypass safety alignments. The paper responds to limitations in existing multi-turn benchmarks that rely heavily on templates and lack scale or diversity. It combines active learning approaches from machine learning with adversarial prompt generation, drawing on uncertainty sampling techniques to guide iterative improvement. The work also connects to broader safety evaluation literature that emphasizes the importance of comprehensive benchmarking for understanding model vulnerabilities across diverse attack vectors and harmful categories.
enables: This benchmark enables more rigorous evaluation and improvement of LLM safety mechanisms by providing diverse, high-quality multi-turn adversarial examples. Future work can leverage MultiBreak to develop more robust safety-alignment techniques that specifically address conversational jailbreaks rather than isolated prompts. The active learning pipeline approach can be extended to generate attacks in new domains or against emerging LLM architectures. The dataset itself supports comparative studies of different safety training methods and the identification of failure modes across model families. Additionally, the diversity of harmful intents and topics creates opportunities for analyzing which safety mechanisms generalize across categories and which require domain-specific improvements.
